content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

July 5, 1977

Constantien P. Matthews
Attorney at Law
Blackstone Building
366 North Arrowhead Avenue
San Bernardino, California 92401

Dear Mr. Matthews:

This letter is to confirm your inquiry as to whether
or not it is a proper expenditure of campaign funds to
pay legal fees to opposing counsel or to counsel of
Assemblyman Terry Goggin. The Political Reform Act con-
tains no restrictions on the use of campaign money and
thus campaign funds may be used for any expenditure.

While I am not aware of any statutes governing the
use of campaign funds, it appears clear that campaign
funds must be used for the purposes of furthering the
candidacy of the candidate. Thus if a campaign used the
funds for a purpose totally unrelated to the campaign or
candidate (e.g., a trip to Hawaii) then it is entirely
possible that campaign contributors would have a cause of
action against the campaign for improper use of funds.
However, I am aware of no such lawsuits brought by campaign
contributors.

Please feel free to call on me if you have any
further questions.

Sincerely,

Robert M. Stern
General Counsel

RMS:nc

C. P. "TINO" MATTHEWS
ATTORNEY AT LAW
BLACKSTONE BUILDING

366 NORTH ARROWHEAD AVENUE
SAN BERNARDINO, CALIF. 92401

PHONES 714/860-6813 - 714/889-6814

June 14, 1977


Robert M. Stern, Esq.,
General Counsel,
Fair Political Practices Commission,
State of California
P.O. Box 807,
Sacramento, California

Re: Our conversation of May 25, 1977.

Dear Mr. Stern:

I would like to thank you for your courtesy in seeing me on
the morning of May 25, 1977 and discussing the law suit
in which Assemblyman Terry Goggin is being sued pursuant
to the provisions of Government Code Sections 3600 to
3760. Our conversation was most enlighting and helpful.

The second purpose of this letter is to confirm your
opinion that it would be a proper expenditure of campaign
funds, on the part of any committee to elect or reelect
Terry Goggin to the assembly or other political office,
to pay legal fees to opposing counsel or Mr. Goggins counsel
for time spent and in settlement of a law suit brought
pursuant to Government Code Section 3600 to 3760.

I am wondering if you would be so kind as to forward to me
a short note again confirming the propriety of this type
of expenditure.

Thank you very much for your kind consideration in the matter
and you able assistance.

Sincerely yours,


CONSTANTIEN P. MATTHEWS

3760. Our conversation was most enlightning and helpful.

sections.question: Whether or not it is a proper expenditure of campaign funds to pay legal fees to opposing counsel or to counsel of Assemblyman Terry Goggin.
sections.conclusion: The Political Reform Act contains no restrictions on the use of campaign money and thus campaign funds may be used for any expenditure. While I am not aware of any statutes governing the use of campaign funds, it appears clear that campaign funds must be used for the purposes of furthering the candidacy of the candidate. Thus if a campaign used the funds for a purpose totally unrelated to the campaign or candidate (e.g., a trip to Hawaii) then it is entirely possible that campaign contributors would have a cause of action against the campaign for improper use of funds.
sections.question_synthetic: The inquiry asks whether campaign funds may be used to pay legal fees to opposing counsel or to Assemblyman Goggin's counsel in connection with a lawsuit brought under Government Code Sections 3600-3760.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that the Political Reform Act contains no restrictions on campaign fund use, and thus campaign funds may be used for any expenditure so long as it furthers the candidate's candidacy. Legal fees related to the lawsuit would likely qualify as a proper campaign expenditure if connected to the candidate's position or campaign.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (dated June 14, 1977) and the FPPC response letter (dated July 5, 1977). The response letter is somewhat fragmented in the provided text but contains the key legal opinion. High confidence in core extraction, though document quality affected confidence slightly.

QUESTION: Whether or not it is a proper expenditure of campaign funds to pay legal fees to opposing counsel or to counsel of Assemblyman Terry Goggin.

CONCLUSION: The Political Reform Act contains no restrictions on the use of campaign money and thus campaign funds may be used for any expenditure. While I am not aware of any statutes governing the use of campaign funds, it appears clear that campaign funds must be used for the purposes of furthering the candidacy of the candidate. Thus if a campaign used the funds for a purpose totally unrelated to the campaign or candidate (e.g., a trip to Hawaii) then it is entirely possible that campaign contributors would have a cause of action against the campaign for improper use of funds.

embedding.summary: An attorney asks the FPPC whether campaign funds may be used to pay legal fees in a lawsuit involving Assemblyman Terry Goggin. The FPPC General Counsel advises that the Political Reform Act does not restrict campaign fund use provided expenditures further the candidate's candidacy.
